title: A Beginner's Guide to Apache Airflow 3
summary: Apache Airflow provides a programmatic approach to orchestrating data workflows through Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) and Tasks, enabling data pipelines to manage task dependencies and execution timing. Unlike cron jobs which blindly execute at scheduled times regardless of task completion, Airflow ensures tasks run only after their dependencies complete, preventing data corruption in ETL processes.
content:
If the terms orchestration or Apache Airflow sound like intimidating industry jargon, this article will help you cut through the noise and understand the basics.
data orchestration? In DataOps (Data Operations), it is the underlying system that manages data workflows (such as ETL pipelines) to ensure tasks run at the right time and in the correct sequence.
What is a DAG? A DAG is a model that contains all the tasks to be run. DAG stands for:
Directed meaning tasks have a specific direction.
Acyclic meaning it has no circular dependencies — extraction cannot depend on transformation if transformation depends on extraction.
Graph meaning a collection of tasks (nodes) connected by dependencies (edges).
What is a Task? This is a step in a DAG that describes a single unit of work.

Think of the DAG as an orchestra conductor and the tasks as the instruments. 
The Scheduler submits tasks to the executor and triggers scheduled workflows.
A DAG processor reads DAG files and organizes them in the metadata database.
The Webserver is the Airflow User Interface for inspecting, triggering and debugging the behaviour of DAGs and tasks.
A dedicated folder of DAG files which contains the DAG, is read by the scheduler to figure out which tasks to run and when to run them.
The Metadata Database stores the state of tasks, DAGs and variables.
At this point you might be asking yourself, Why not just use cron jobs? Well, think of cron jobs as an alarm clock and Airflow as a project manager. Cron just runs your script at a certain time with no regard for the task's dependencies. 
extract.py for 12:00 AM and transform.py for 1:30 AM. If extraction takes 40 minutes, Cron will blindly trigger the transformation at 1:30 AM, leading to corrupted data or a crash. 
from airflow.sdk import DAG 
from airflow.providers.standard.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.providers.standard.operators.bash import BashOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta 

# Step 1: Define your Pyt

zh.title: Apache Airflow 3 初学者指南
zh.summary: Apache Airflow 通过有向无环图（DAG）和任务提供了一种编程方式来协调数据工作流，使数据管道能够管理任务依赖关系和执行时间。与无论任务是否完成都盲目按计划时间执行的 cron 作业不同，Airflow 确保任务只有在其依赖项完成后才能运行，防止 ETL 流程中的数据损坏。

title: 12 Phrases Your ATS Is Quietly Penalizing — Found by Running 200 Resumes Through 5 Systems
summary: Analysis of 200 resumes processed through five ATS systems identified 12 phrases that consistently reduce screening scores by at least 4 percentile points, including 'responsible for,' 'helped with,' and vague descriptors like 'various.' These problematic phrases fall into three categories: filler verbs that obscure accomplishments, vague nouns that produce no keyword overlap with job descriptions, and unsubstantiated soft skill claims.
content:
The follow-up I owed myself. Last week I reverse-engineered 5 ATS systems and that post hit harder than I expected, partly because it gave readers a mental model but mostly because it made them ask the obvious follow-up question:
Okay, but which words specifically should I delete from my resume?
So I ran another batch — 200 anonymized resumes through 5 different ATS-style scoring pipelines (3 commercial, 2 open-source replicas tuned to match the commercial outputs) and looked for phrases that consistently dropped the score relative to a baseline. Here are the 12 worst offenders.
For each resume I ran two passes: original, and a "phrase-scrubbed" copy where I removed only the candidate phrase and replaced it with a neutral synonym from a controlled list. I logged the score delta. The phrases below produced an average score drop of at least 4 percentile points across all 5 systems, and at least one system flagged the resume into a lower screening tier.
1. "Responsible for"
2. "Helped with" / "Assisted with"
3. "Various" (as in "various technologies", "various clients")
4. "Synergize" / "Synergies"
5. "Innovative solutions"
6. "Team player"
7. "Strong communication skills"
8. "Familiar with [tool]"
9. "Hard worker" / "Detail-oriented"
10. "Multitask" / "Multitasking"
11. "References available upon request"
12. "Proven track record"
If you read the list back to back, the same three failure modes show up:
Filler verbs that hide the real verb. Responsible for, helped with, assisted with. Get rid of the helper verb and lead with the action.
Vague nouns that match nothing in the job description. Various, multiple, several. The ATS scores keyword overlap; vague nouns produce no overlap.
Self-claimed soft skills with no evidence. Team player, hard worker, strong communicator, detail-oriented. None of them match anything the JD asked for.
Pull up your resume and search for those 12 phrases. Removing them takes maybe 20 minutes. The systems I tested moved an average resume up a

zh.title: 12 个短语正被你的 ATS 系统偷偷降分 — 通过 5 个系统处理 200 份简历发现
zh.summary: 对通过五个 ATS 系统处理的 200 份简历的分析发现了 12 个短语，这些短语将筛选得分降低至少 4 个百分点，包括"负责"、"协助"和模糊描述符如"各种"。这些有问题的短语分为三类：隐藏成就的填充动词、与职位描述没有关键词重叠的模糊名词，以及未经证实的软技能声明。

title: Very Inspiring
summary: AI coding agents perform most effectively when working within well-structured codebases that provide clear context and organization, rather than being limited by the underlying language model's capabilities. The concept of an 'AI harness'—proper repository setup with documentation, clear architecture, and consistent patterns—can transform an agent's effectiveness from producing fabricated code to shipping production-ready features.
content:
The AI Harness: why your AI coding agent is only as smart as the repo you put it in

title: Do Workshop Papers at NeurIPS/ICML Actually Help Your PhD Application? Here's What Admissions Committees Actually See
summary: Workshop papers from conferences like NeurIPS and ICML can significantly influence PhD admissions outcomes, but their value depends on positioning and broader application context. A workshop paper serves as a signal amplifier rather than a standalone credential, strengthening applications that already demonstrate research capability while providing limited benefit to weaker candidates. Applicants should understand the informal prestige hierarchy of ML venues and strategically position workshop papers.
content:
TL;DR: You got accepted to the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Robustness in Foundation Models.  Congrats — that's genuinely hard.
📖 Reading time: ~28 min
The Problem: You Have a Workshop Paper and You Don't Know What It's Worth
First, Let's Be Honest About What a Workshop Paper Is (and Isn't)
The Conference Tier Breakdown: Which Workshops Actually Move the Needle
How to Actually List Workshop Papers on Your CV Without Overselling or Underselling
The SOP Section: This Is Where Workshop Papers Either Help or Hurt You
When a Workshop Paper Is a Net Negative on Your Application
The 2026 Context: How the ML PhD Market Has Changed Workshop Paper Value
Practical Checklist: Before You Submit Your 2026 PhD Application
You got accepted to the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Robustness in Foundation Models. Congrats — that's genuinely hard. You spent three months on this paper, got real feedback from reviewers, and made it into a venue where serious researchers will see your work. Now you're staring at the "Publications" section of your PhD application and wondering if listing it will help you or silently mark you as someone who doesn't know how academia works. That uncertainty is the actual problem, and it's more common than anyone admits out loud.
The Reddit threads aren't overreacting. I've seen "does a workshop paper count?" posts on r/MachineLearning and r/gradadmissions hit the front page repeatedly, because the anxiety is legitimate — nobody in undergrad explicitly teaches you the informal prestige hierarchy of ML venues. You didn't go to a school with a strong ML lab. Your advisor is in a related field and says "it's good, list it." But you've seen people online say workshop papers are worthless, and other people say they got into top-5 programs with nothing but workshop papers. Both things are true for different people in different situations, which is exactly why generic advice fails here.
Here's my actual take: a workshop paper is a signal amplifier, not a signal by itself. It

zh.title: 研讨会论文真的能帮助你的博士申请吗？这是招生委员会实际看到的
zh.summary: 来自NeurIPS和ICML等会议的研讨会论文可以显著影响博士申请结果，但其价值取决于定位和更广泛的申请背景。研讨会论文充当信号放大器而非独立凭证，强化已展示研究能力的申请，对较弱申请帮助有限。申请者应理解机器学习会议的非正式声望等级，并战略性地定位研讨会论文。

title: How to Optimize Python 3.12 Code with Cython 3 and Rust 1.85 Bindings for 10x Speedups
summary: Python 3.12 code can achieve 10-14x performance improvements through Cython 3 static typing and Rust 1.85 FFI bindings, as demonstrated by matrix multiplication benchmarks showing speedups from 12.7 seconds (pure Python) to 0.89 seconds (Rust). Cython 3 provides full Python 3.12 support with improved GIL release primitives, while Rust's pyo3 crate reduces binding boilerplate by 40%.
content:
Python’s global interpreter lock (GIL) and dynamic typing make it 10–100x slower than compiled languages for CPU-bound workloads. In 2024 benchmarks, a naive Python 3.12 matrix multiplication function took 12.7 seconds to process 1000x1000 float arrays, while a Cython 3-optimized version ran in 1.4 seconds, and a Rust 1.85 binding dropped that to 0.89 seconds — a 14.3x speedup over pure Python, with zero Python code changes for the Rust path.
  ⭐ rust-lang/rust — 112,466 stars, 14,875 forks
  ⭐ python/cpython — 72,543 stars, 34,529 forks
Data pulled live from GitHub and npm.
  Ti-84 Evo (232 points)
  New research suggests people can communicate and practice skills while dreaming (207 points)
  The smelly baby problem (67 points)
  Eka’s robotic claw feels like we're approaching a ChatGPT moment (75 points)
  Ask HN: Who is hiring? (May 2026) (218 points)
  Matrix multiplication workloads see 8–14x speedups when optimized with Cython 3 static typing and Rust 1.85 FFI bindings, benchmarked against Python 3.12.2 on an M2 Max CPU.
  Cython 3.0.5 adds full Python 3.12 support, including match statements and improved GIL release primitives for zero-overhead parallelism.
  Rust 1.85’s pyo3 0.20 crate reduces binding boilerplate by 40% compared to 1.70, with automatic GIL management for Python 3.12 compatibility.
  By 2026, 60% of performance-critical Python libraries will ship Rust or Cython extensions as default, up from 22% in 2023, per PyPI download metrics.
Before optimizing, we need a reproducible baseline to measure gains against. We’ll implement a naive matrix multiplication function in pure Python 3.12, with validation, benchmarking, and correctness checks. This code will run without any external dependencies (beyond the standard library) to isolate Python’s native performance.

import time
import sys
from typing import List, Tuple, Optional

def validate_matrix(mat: List[List[float]], name: str) -> None:
    """Validate that a matrix is non-empty and rectangular.

zh.title: 如何使用Cython 3和Rust 1.85绑定优化Python 3.12代码以实现10倍加速
zh.summary: Python 3.12代码可通过Cython 3静态类型和Rust 1.85 FFI绑定实现10-14倍性能提升，矩阵乘法基准测试证明从12.7秒（纯Python）加速到0.89秒（Rust）。Cython 3提供完整Python 3.12支持并改进了GIL释放原语，Rust的pyo3库将绑定样板代码减少40%。

title: The AI Harness: why your AI coding agent is only as smart as the repo you put it in
summary: An open-source team documented how codebase quality and organization—the 'AI harness'—determines whether AI agents can effectively contribute to real production systems with multiple components and third-party integrations. By implementing proper documentation, clear architecture, consistent patterns, and test infrastructure, the team moved from agents inventing non-existent entities to shipping production-ready features. This reveals that AI agent effectiveness is primarily constrained by repository structure rather than language model capability.
content:
Hi. I am an engineer at Activepieces, an open source automation platform (think Zapier, but you can self host it and read the source code). I write TypeScript for a living. I argue with bots about tabs vs spaces now. And our team spent the last few months really, really invested in one question:
Why does our AI coding agent suck in this codebase, but cook so hard in a blank Next.js project on my laptop?
Spoiler: it is not the model. It is never the model. It is me. It is the repo. It is the thing I will spend the next ~6,000 words trying to convince you to call an AI harness.
Here is the claim. I want to be careful with it, because the internet is tired of "we 10x'd productivity with AI" hype posts. I am tired of them too.
We have 12+ engineers at Activepieces. Our internal goal is one feature per engineer per day, ideally from a single prompt. We have not hit that goal yet. But we are moving fast toward it, and the speed of progress is what is actually interesting.
We are an open source team trying to figure out, in public, how to set up a real codebase so a frontier model can actually be useful in it.
Our codebase is real. Multi tenant. Multi edition. We have an enterprise tier, a cloud product, a frontend, a backend, an engine, a piece SDK, and integrations with hundreds of third party APIs. So this is not a toy.
This post is the answer we have so far. It is not the final answer. It is the answer that took us from "Claude Code keeps inventing entities that do not exist" to "Claude Code just shipped a credentials manager: a solid plan, two iterations, and the feature was production ready."
Let me show you how.
💡 Reading time check: This is a long post. Around 30 minutes. There is a lot of stuff. I would rather give you the full picture than send you off with a half formed mental model that breaks the moment you try to use it. Grab coffee. Or skip to the section called "Anatomy of an AI Harness" if you want the meat.

Let me start with the part of AI coding talk t

zh.title: AI工具库：你的AI编码代理只能与你放入的仓库一样聪明
zh.summary: 一个开源团队记录了代码库质量和组织（"AI工具库"）如何决定AI代理能否有效地为具有多个组件和第三方集成的真实生产系统做出贡献。通过实施适当的文档、清晰的架构、一致的模式和测试基础设施，该团队从代理发明不存在的实体转变为交付生产就绪的功能。这揭示了AI代理的有效性主要由仓库结构而非语言模型能力所制约。

title: Images, Fonts, Third-Party Scripts: LCP and CLS
summary: Images, fonts, and third-party scripts are responsible for the majority of field Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) regressions, with interactions between them amplifying performance degradation. Optimization techniques include using fetchpriority attributes and preload directives for hero images, selecting appropriate image formats, implementing font-display strategies, and using the facade pattern to defer third-party script execution until user interaction.
content: Images, fonts, and third-party scripts are the three categories responsible for the most field LCP and CLS regressions. They interact: a 12KB GTM tag firing synchronously in blocks the parser for 200ms, which means your hero image (already the LCP candidate) now loads 200ms later. LCP jumps from 1.8s to 4.2s. CrUX data reflects it three weeks later. What this covers: fetchpriority and for LCP images, WebP vs AVIF trade-offs, srcset/sizes resolution selection, font-display values and what each costs, font subsetting, and the facade pattern that eliminates third-party script cost entirely until user interaction. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures when the largest visible element in the viewport finishes rendering. In most marketing sites, e-commerce pages, and landing pages, that element is an image: specifically the hero image above the fold. The browser's default behavior for images is loading="lazy", which means it defers fetching images until they're near the viewport. For images below the fold, this is exactly right. For the LCP image, it is catastrophic. ![Hero](https://renderlog.in/hero.jpg) ![Hero](https://renderlog.in/hero.webp) fetchpriority="high" is the piece most developers miss. Even with loading="eager", the browser's resource prioritization algorithm might assign the image a lower network priority if it's discovered late in the HTML parse: for example, if it's inside a JS-rendered component. fetchpriority="high" overrides this and tells the browser: this resource competes with critical CSS, treat it accordingly. The most aggressive technique is a in the document head. This fires before the HTML parser even reaches the tag:

zh.title: 图片、字体、第三方脚本：LCP 和 CLS
zh.summary: 图片、字体和第三方脚本导致大部分字段最大内容绘制（LCP）和累积布局偏移（CLS）性能下降，它们之间的相互作用进一步加剧了性能恶化。优化技术包括为关键图片使用 fetchpriority 属性和预加载指令、选择适当的图片格式、实施字体显示策略，以及使用外观模式延迟第三方脚本执行直到用户交互。

title: Deploying Blob Storage for a Public website on Azure
summary: Azure Blob Storage provides cloud-based unstructured data storage suitable for public website asset hosting, configured with read-access geo-redundant storage for high availability and anonymous blob-level read access. The setup involves creating a storage account, configuring redundancy settings, creating public containers, and enabling anonymous access permissions. This configuration supports secure public file distribution while maintaining durability through geographic redundancy.
content:
Table of Content



What is Blob Storage?
Create a storage account with high availability.
Create a blob storage container with anonymous read access
Practice uploading files and testing access.
Configure soft delete
Configure blob versioning
Conclusion

Blob Storage is a cloud storage solution used for storing large amount of unstructured data including text documents, emails, social media posts, multimedia (audio/video/images) Learn more.  

In the portal, search for and select Storage accounts.

Select + Create.

For resource group select new. Give your resource group a name and select OK.
Set the Storage account name to publicwebsite. Make sure the storage account name is unique by adding an identifier.
Take the defaults for other settings.
Select Review and then Create.

Wait for the storage account to deploy, and then select Go to resource.

In the storage account, in the Data management section, select the Redundancy blade.
Ensure Read-access Geo-redundant storage is selected.
Review the primary and secondary location information.

In the storage account, in the Settings section, select the Configuration blade.
Ensure the Allow blob anonymous access setting is Enabled.
Be sure to Save your changes.


In your storage account, in the Data storage section, select the Containers blade.
Select + Container.
Ensure the Name of the container is public.
Select Create.

Select your public container.
On the Overview blade, select Change access level.

Ensure the Public access level is Blob (anonymous read access for blobs only).
Select OK.


public container. The type of file doesn’t matter. A small image or text file is a good choice.



Ensure you are viewing your container.
Select Upload.
Browse to files and select a file. Browse to a file of your choice.
Select Upload.
Close the upload window, Refresh the page and ensure your file was uploaded.

Select your uploaded file.
On the Overview tab, copy the URL.

Paste the URL into a new browser tab.
If you have uploaded

zh.summary: Azure Blob 存储提供基于云的非结构化数据存储，适合公共网站资产托管，配置了读取访问地理冗余存储以实现高可用性和匿名 Blob 级读取访问。设置涉及创建存储账户、配置冗余设置、创建公共容器和启用匿名访问权限。此配置支持安全的公共文件分发，同时通过地理冗余保持持久性。

title: Taming the Chaos: Cleaning 10M+ Apple Health Records into a Production-Ready Parquet Lakehouse
summary: Transforming Apple Health's bloated XML exports into production-ready formats requires a robust data pipeline handling duplicate entries from multiple devices and inconsistent sampling frequencies across 10+ million records. Using Polars for high-performance DataFrame operations, Apache Hop for metadata-driven workflow orchestration, and S3 for distributed storage creates a scalable architecture that processes data without excessive RAM consumption.
content:
If you’ve ever tried to click that "Export Health Data" button on your iPhone, you know the feeling of pure dread that follows. You expect a clean CSV; you get a bloated, multi-gigabyte XML file that looks like it was designed by a chaotic deity. 
When building high-performance AI models for health tech, Apple Health data is a goldmine—but only if you can navigate the minefield of data engineering challenges. We’re talking about massive data volumes, duplicate entries from overlapping devices (iPhone vs. Apple Watch), and inconsistent sampling frequencies that would make any data scientist cry. 
In this tutorial, we are going to build a robust Data Pipeline using Polars, Apache Hop, and S3 to transform "dirty" XML exports into a standardized, high-performance Parquet Lakehouse. 
Pro-Tip: If you are looking for advanced architectural patterns for health-tech scaling, I highly recommend checking out the production-ready examples over at WellAlly's Engineering Blog.
Before we dive into the code, let's look at the flow. We need a system that can handle millions of rows without blowing up your RAM.
graph TD
    A[Apple Health Export XML] --> B[Apache Hop: Ingestion]
    B --> C[S3 Raw Landing Zone]
    C --> D[Polars: Data Cleaning & De-duplication]
    D --> E[Outlier Removal & Resampling]
    E --> F[Standardized Parquet Lakehouse]
    F --> G[Downstream AI/ML Models]

    subgraph "The Processing Core"
    D
    E
    end

To follow along, you’ll need:
Python 3.9+
Polars: The lightning-fast DataFrame library.
Apache Hop: For workflow orchestration.
S3 Bucket: To act as our data lake storage.
While we love Python, using Apache Hop for the initial ingestion allows us to handle the XML-to-S3 transfer visually and reliably. Hop handles the metadata-driven workflow, ensuring that if the upload fails halfway through, we can resume.
Create a "Get XML" transform.
Use the "S3 File Output" to land the raw XML into an s3://raw-zone/.
Why Polars? Because parsing 10 million rows o

zh.title: 驯服混乱：将 1000 万+ 条 Apple Health 记录清洁为生产就绪的 Parquet Lakehouse
zh.summary: 将 Apple Health 臃肿的 XML 导出转换为生产就绪格式需要强大的数据管道来处理来自多个设备的重复条目和跨 1000 万+ 条记录的不一致采样频率。使用 Polars 进行高性能 DataFrame 操作、Apache Hop 进行元数据驱动的工作流编排以及 S3 进行分布式存储，创建了一个可扩展的架构，能够处理数据而无需过度消耗 RAM。

title: Why Most Crypto Bots Get Sandwiched (And How to Prevent It)
summary: Sandwich attacks in decentralized finance occur when attackers manipulate transaction ordering in the public mempool by front-running a victim's transaction with higher gas fees, executing their own profitable transaction, and back-running after execution. Most crypto trading bots remain vulnerable because they operate in public mempools with predictable behavior and low gas fees, providing attackers sufficient time to detect and exploit opportunities.
content:
Why Most Crypto Bots Get Sandwiched (And How to Prevent It)


If you’ve ever built or run a crypto trading bot, you’ve likely encountered the dreaded "sandwich attack." It’s one of the most frustrating and costly issues in decentralized finance (DeFi) trading. In this article, I’ll explain what sandwich attacks are, how they work, why your bot is vulnerable, and—most importantly—how to protect yourself using tools like Jito bundles. I’ll also share some real-world numbers and practical lessons I’ve learned the hard way.
A sandwich attack is a form of Miner Extractable Value (MEV) where an attacker exploits your transaction by placing their own transaction before and after yours. Here’s how it works:
Front-Running: The attacker detects your pending transaction (e.g., a swap on Uniswap) and submits their own transaction with a higher gas fee to execute first.
Execution: Your transaction executes in the middle, often at a worse price due to the attacker’s manipulation.
Back-Running: The attacker closes their position by executing another transaction immediately after yours, profiting from the price movement they created.
For example, if your bot tries to buy ETH with DAI, an attacker can buy ETH first (driving the price up), allow your transaction to execute at the inflated price, and then sell ETH immediately after, pocketing the difference.
Most crypto bots are vulnerable to sandwich attacks because they operate in public mempools. Transactions sit in the mempool before being included in a block, and attackers use sophisticated tools to scan for profitable opportunities. Here are the key reasons:
Low Gas Fees: Bots often use low or average gas fees to save costs, making them susceptible to being front-run.
Predictable Behavior: Bots often follow simple strategies (e.g., buying when a price hits a threshold), making their transactions easy to anticipate.
Limited Protection: Many bot developers don’t implement MEV protection mechanisms, leaving their transactions expos

zh.title: 为什么大多数加密机器人容易遭受夹击攻击（以及如何防止）
zh.summary: 去中心化金融中的夹击攻击是指攻击者通过提高燃气费用抢先执行受害者交易，然后执行自己的有利可图的交易，最后进行回追的方式来操纵公共内存池中的交易顺序。大多数加密交易机器人易于被攻击，因为它们在具有可预测行为和低燃气费用的公共内存池中运行，给攻击者充足的时间来检测和利用机会。

title: Devstral 2: Run Mistral's Open Coding Agent Locally
summary: Mistral's Devstral 2 achieves 72.2% accuracy on SWE-bench Verified—matching Claude Sonnet 4.5's performance—while running entirely on-premise on a single RTX 4090 GPU or Mac with 32GB RAM. The model costs 7x less per token than Claude Sonnet 4.5 and was trained to excel at real multi-file, multi-step engineering tasks. This approach trades five percentage points of accuracy for 85% cost savings.
content:
Proprietary coding agents are powerful, but every token you send to a cloud API is a token that leaves your codebase. Devstral 2, released December 9, 2025 by Mistral AI, changes that calculus. At 72.2% on SWE-bench Verified — the industry-standard benchmark for real GitHub issue resolution — it matches Claude Sonnet 4.5's general performance territory while running fully on-premise on a single RTX 4090 or a Mac with 32GB RAM.
This guide covers what Devstral 2 and Devstral Small 2 are, their specs and benchmarks, how to pull and run the smaller variant via Ollama, and how to wire either model into your daily coding workflow.
Effloow Lab verified Ollama compatibility (v0.20.5), confirmed registry tags, and documented hardware requirements before writing this guide. See data/lab-runs/devstral-2-mistral-coding-agent-local-guide-2026.md for the full evidence record.
Most open-weight coding models have a dirty secret: they score well on toy benchmarks but fall apart on real multi-file, multi-step engineering tasks. SWE-bench Verified uses 500 manually screened GitHub issues from production repositories — not hand-curated puzzles. It measures whether an agent can actually read a codebase, reason about the bug, make targeted edits across multiple files, and produce a passing test run.
Devstral 2's 72.2% on that benchmark is not a coincidence. Mistral built the model in collaboration with All Hands AI, the team behind OpenHands — a production agentic framework used for real software engineering workflows. The training process was specifically designed around multi-step tool use: reading files, editing code, running tests, and iterating based on output.
For teams running high-volume coding tasks, the cost arithmetic is compelling. Claude Sonnet 4.5, which scores 77.2% on the same benchmark, costs $3/$15 per million tokens (input/output). Devstral 2 API pricing is $0.40/$2.00 — roughly 7x cheaper per token. Trading five percentage points of accuracy for 85% cost savings is a

zh.title: Devstral 2：在本地运行Mistral的开源编码代理
zh.summary: Mistral的Devstral 2在SWE-bench Verified上达到72.2%的准确率——与Claude Sonnet 4.5性能相当——同时完全本地运行，支持单个RTX 4090 GPU或32GB RAM的Mac。该模型的每个token成本比Claude Sonnet 4.5低7倍，专门为多文件、多步骤工程任务而训练。此方案以牺牲5个百分点的准确率换取85%的成本节省。

title: OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs. Datadog 2026: Tracing Overhead for 1000 RPS Microservices Workloads Measured
summary: A benchmark of 1000 RPS microservices workloads shows OpenTelemetry 1.25 introduces 35-45% lower tracing overhead compared to Datadog 2026 across latency, CPU, and memory metrics. Both systems maintained >99.98% trace export success rates under sustained load, though OpenTelemetry achieved slightly lower export latency. These results demonstrate that distributed tracing overhead is quantifiable and tool selection significantly impacts production microservices performance.
content:
OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs Datadog 2026: Tracing Overhead for 1000 RPS Microservices Workloads


Distributed tracing is critical for debugging microservices, but instrumentation overhead can degrade production performance. This benchmark compares OpenTelemetry (OTel) 1.25 and Datadog’s 2026 tracing stack under a sustained 1000 requests per second (RPS) microservices workload to quantify real-world overhead.
We deployed a 3-service e-commerce microservices stack on Kubernetes (1.29):
  Frontend: Node.js 20, handles user requests
  Backend: Go 1.22, processes business logic
  Database: PostgreSQL 16, persists data
Load was generated via k6 at a constant 1000 RPS for 60 minutes. We measured overhead with 100% trace sampling to isolate instrumentation impact, with no other observability tools running. Key metrics:
  Request latency (p50, p95, p99) with and without tracing
  Per-pod CPU and memory utilization
  Trace export success rate and export latency
We used the OTel SDK for Node.js and Go, with the OTLP gRPC exporter sending traces to a local OpenTelemetry Collector 0.90. Sampling was set to 100% (always_on). No additional processors or extensions were enabled to minimize external overhead.
We installed the Datadog Agent 7.55 (2026 GA release) with tracing enabled. The Datadog Node.js and Go tracing libraries were used, with 100% sampling matching the OTel configuration. Default Datadog tagging (service, env, version) was left enabled.
All tests were run 3 times, with results averaged. Baseline (no tracing) latency: p50=12ms, p95=45ms, p99=89ms.
Tool

p99 Overhead (ms)

  OpenTelemetry 1.25: 99.992% export success, average export latency 12ms
  Datadog 2026: 99.989% export success, average export latency 18ms
OpenTelemetry 1.25 showed 35-45% lower overhead across all metrics. This aligns with

zh.title: OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs. Datadog 2026：1000 RPS微服务工作负载追踪开销测量
zh.summary: 对1000 RPS微服务工作负载的基准测试显示，OpenTelemetry 1.25在延迟、CPU和内存指标上相比Datadog 2026的追踪开销降低35-45%。两个系统在持续负载下都保持99.98%以上的追踪导出成功率，尽管OpenTelemetry的导出延迟略低。这些结果表明分布式追踪开销可量化，工具选择对生产微服务性能影响重大。
